Name: Micro Gravity Logistics
Description:
Innovation is never guaranteed. It is fought for—by people, for people.
But as long as humanity continues to fight for innovation through bold engineering and the courage to build, we believe our progression through the Kardashev Scale is inevitable.

We’re building scalable and efficient robotic systems for autonomous material handling in space—laying the foundation for a multi-planetary economy within this century.

Our first step is enabling pharmaceutical manufacturing in orbit, which we believe will become the first non-subsidised, fully commercial industry in space. To make this possible, we’re developing robotic infrastructure capable of transferring materials from launch vehicles into production modules and intermediary storage with precision, reliability, and autonomy. These systems are anchored within a space station–like structure, providing the stability and modularity needed to support industrial-scale operations in microgravity.

We’re not just building for today.
We’re building for the next phase of civilization.

Name: The Quantum Archaeoastronomy Institute of Brazil
Description:
The Quantum Archaeoastronomy Institute of Brazil (QAIB) is a regenerative research and intelligence network linking science, design, and open technology to the living systems of Earth. Rooted in Brazil and aligned with NASA GLOBE, UNOOSA’s Open Universe, AEB, and INPE, QAIB bridges citizen science with frontier innovation to build a new civic model for sustainable development.

QAIB operates as both institute and ecosystem — a public intelligence agency for the planet. Our work spans renewable energy design, biocultural restoration, open data infrastructure, and adaptive AI. Each project — from Vila Qatuan’s regenerative field lab to the Savanno garden-space — acts as a living demonstration of systems thinking in action, where culture, energy, and ecology evolve together.

Through the Intersectoral Alliance for Regenerative Intelligence (IARI), QAIB connects researchers, artists, engineers, and institutions in a shared commitment to open science and inclusive innovation. The goal is not just to study the future, but to prototype it — creating frameworks where technology serves life rather than extracting from it.

QAIB’s guiding principle is simple: intelligence is a living field. By aligning human creativity with planetary dynamics, we aim to regenerate environments, economies, and communities — one field, one project, one conversation at a time.

 Navigating the Future — Bridging Time, Science, and Sustainability.

Name: Ember Space
Description:
Ember Space is a consultancy specialising in space thermal engineering, with work conducted by Clint Therakam, an experienced thermal engineer. Ember Space provides end-to-end thermal design, modelling, analysis, and evaluation of space systems, payloads, satellites, and vehicles.

With over seven years of expertise in space thermal, mechanical, materials, and Assembly, Integration, and Testing (AIT) engineering, Clint has played a pivotal role in the development and launch of four successful Australian satellites as well as numerous space studies and consultancy projects. His hands-on experience in mechanical and AIT engineering greatly enhances his thermal engineering solutions. Recently, Clint was the lead thermal engineer for the SpIRIT nanosatellite, where he designed, modelled, manufactured, and tested thermal payloads and systems during all mission phases - including operations. Clint was also the lead thermal engineer for the ELO2 consortium's lunar rover for the flagship Australian Trailblazer program during stage 1. Clint worked on the rover's early and preliminary thermal design where he refined various high-level designs and parameters that ultimately led to his PDR-level thermal design.

In the last two years, Clint was the sole thermal consultant at the University of Melbourne's Melbourne Space Laboratory (MSL) where he provided thermal consulting services to various Australian and international space missions and payloads, including:
- Thermal design and low-fidelity modelling of lunar spacecraft systems (with Lunar Outpost Luxembourg),
- Nanosatellite thermal modelling and analysis (with Myriota),
- Small satellite thermal design, modelling and analysis as well as Multilayer Insulation (MLI) design and manufacture (with Space Machines), and
- Bespoke payload thermal modelling for thermal clocks (QuantX).

Name: Morocco in Space
Description:
Executive Summary

Morocco is entering a transformative era by building a national space ecosystem capable of training astronauts, conducting research, growing industry, and inspiring future generations. This strategic framework outlines the pillars of the ecosystem, the impacted sectors, and a 10‑year roadmap integrating human spaceflight, research, STEM development, industrial capability, and policy evolution. The Morocco in Space Foundation acts as the national catalyst, enabling partnerships, coordinating institutions, and accelerating Morocco’s emergence as a spacefaring nation.

Vision & Mission

Vision:
Enable Morocco to become a recognized spacefaring nation contributing to global exploration, science, and innovation. 

Mission:
Build national capacity by connecting Moroccan institutions with global partners, supporting astronaut development, enabling microgravity research, expanding STEM education, emerging space manufacturing, and advising the government on long‑term space policy and agency formation. 
Strategic Pillars

Morocco’s ecosystem is built on 9 core elements:
1. Human Spaceflight
2. Space Research & Microgravity Science
3. STEM Education & Workforce Development
4. Innovation & Startups
5. International Partnerships
6. Space Manufacturing
7. AI & Data Security
8. Policy & Governance Support
9. National Branding & Public Engagement

Global Benchmarking

Leading nations like UAE, Saudi Arabia, USA, Luxembourg, France, and Japan succeeded by aligning government support, global partnerships, academic excellence, and industry growth.
Morocco adapts these principles to build a scalable national program that contributes into the future of spaceflight and science.

Hosted at Cal Poly San Luis Obispo, the birthplace of the CubeSat standard, this event has become a key gathering point for the global small satellite community.

Our mission is to empower the next generation of space pioneers by providing a platform for learning, networking, and driving technological progress in small satellite development. Through a combination of technical sessions, hands-on workshops, and interactive discussions, attendees gain insights into the latest research, industry trends, and technological advancements in the CubeSat ecosystem.

Each year, the workshop covers a broad range of topics, including new developments in CubeSat standards and design, innovations in propulsion, power systems, and communications, alongside educational outreach and workforce development for the space industry.

The CubeSat Developers Workshop is open to all who are passionate about the future of space exploration and small satellite innovation. Whether you’re a seasoned expert or a student entering the field, this workshop provides an invaluable opportunity to connect with thought leaders, share your work, and explore partnerships.

Join us in shaping the future of small satellite technology and empowering the space industry’s innovators!

Connect with Us! Follow this page for updates on the upcoming workshop, speaker announcements, and registration information. Together, we’re advancing space exploration, one CubeSat at a time.

Our mission is to safeguard critical space infrastructure, mitigate collision risks, and empower stakeholders to make informed, proactive decisions in the rapidly evolving space economy.

Harnessing an innovative global hybrid sensor network that integrates optical and radar sensors, we provide unparalleled accuracy in real-time tracking of over 130,000 space objects. Our AI-native analytics platform leverages machine learning to deliver predictive collision assessments, proactive risk management, and seamless communication between terrestrial and orbital assets.

Ground Zero Space addresses critical gaps in current space operations—protecting assets worth billions and ensuring sustainable space activities. With a clear phased approach from ground-based sensors to space-based infrastructure, our scalable solutions are designed to meet the growing demands of satellite operators, defense agencies, insurance providers, and space infrastructure companies.
Our team's commitment to safety, sustainability, and innovation drives our technology-forward approach, creating a safer and more sustainable orbital environment for the global space community.

İstanbul Teknik Üniversitesi’nin 250.yılında kurulan Bellatrix Uzay Takımı, ismini 250 ışık yılı uzaklıktaki Bellatrix yıldızından almaktadır. Bellatrix Uzay Takımı, İTÜ'de kapsamlı bir uzay ekosistemi kurmayı hedeflemektedir. Bu ekosistem kapsamında roverlar, roketler, uydular başta olmak üzere uzay teknolojileri üzerine çalışarak ulusal ve uluslararası yarışmalara katılmayı amaçlamaktadır. Bellatrix, İTÜ'de ve Türkiye'de uzaya ilgiyi artıracak, ulusal ve uluslararası uzay görevlerinde yer alacak nitelikli insanların yetişmesine katkı sağlayacaktır. Bu hedefler kapsamında Nisan 2024’de takım olarak Amerika'da NASA'nın kendi kampüsünde 30 yıldır düzenlediği NASA Human Exploration Rover Challenge'a katılacaktır. HERC, NASA'nın kendisinin organize ettiği uluslararası öğrencilere açık olan tek yarışmasıdır. Bellatrix Uzay Takımı, NASA HERC’e kabul almayı başaran ilk ve tek Türk takımı ünvanını kazanmıştır. 

The Bellatrix Space Team, established in the 250th year of Istanbul Technical University, takes its name from the Bellatrix star, which is located 250 light-years away. The Bellatrix Space Team aims to establish a comprehensive space ecosystem at Istanbul Technical University (ITU). Within this ecosystem, it aims to participate in national and international competitions by working on space technologies, including rovers, rockets, satellites, and etc. Bellatrix will contribute to increasing interest in space at ITU and in Türkiye and will contribute to the training of qualified individuals who will participate in national and international space missions. As part of these goals, in April 2024, as a team, they will participate in the NASA Human Exploration Rover Challenge, which has been organized for 30 years at NASA's own campus in America. HERC is the only competition organized by NASA itself and open to international students. The Bellatrix Space Team has achieved the title of being the first and only Turkish team to be accepted to NASA HERC.

Name: Institute of Space Science - ISS (ROMANIA)
Description:
The main strategy of the ISS is to expand Romanian participation in the worldwide efforts for the peaceful investigation and development of the cosmic space, including applications to improve the quality of life on our planet. We also consider it our mission to preserve and further develop Romanian space capabilities and expertise in order to be able to conduct leading-edge fundamental scientific research.

To implement these goals, the ISS has set very specific scientific and development strategic objectives. Among the scientific objectives are:

Satellite/ground investigations and modelling of solar system plasmas.
Participation to ESC scientific missions (e.g. PLANCK, EUCLID, JUICE,  LISA, etc).
Participation in UHE astrophysical experiments (e.g. ANTARES, KM3NeT, DWARF, Pierre Auger Observatory).
Search for exotic particles and phenomena in cosmic rays and accelerator/collider experiments (e.g. ALICE-CERN, ILC).
Theoretical research of complex structures and processes in astrophysics, gravitation and cosmology.
Development of High Performance Computing resources for high energy elementary particle physics, astrophysics and space science such as GPU computing, cloud computing and GRID sites for development, management and support of large-scale space-borne and ground-based international experiments (e.g. ALICE, PLANCK).
Development of satellite test facilities and of microsatellite design, test and integration facilities (e.g. GOLIAT).
Development of space technology with applications to remote sensing, telemedicine, disaster management and countermeasures for human space flight in adverse conditions.
In terms of development objectives, the ISS is committed to continually improve the quality of its research, expertise and resources, to attract and integrate highly qualified experts, to facilitate the transfer the knowledge and technology for the benefit of society and to encourage the pursuit of partnerships with the industry.

Name: PRE-ECO
Description: Additive manufacturing brought to the emergence of a new class of fibre-reinforced materials; namely, the Variable Angle Tow (VAT) composites. AFP and FDM machines allow the fibres to be relaxed along curvilinear paths within the lamina. In theory, the designer can conceive VAT structures with unexplored capabilities and tailor materials with optimized stiffness-to-weight ratios. In practise, steering brittle fibres, generally made of glass or carbon, is not trivial. Printing must be performed at the right combination of temperature, velocity, curvature radii and pressure to preserve the integrity of fibres. The lack of information on how the effect of these parameters propagates through the scales, from fibres to the final structure, represents the missing piece in the puzzle of VAT composites, which today are either costly or difficult to design because affected by unpredictable failure mechanisms and unwanted defects (gaps, overlaps, and fibre kinking).

Name: Wits Centre for Astrophysics
Description:
The Wits Centre for Astrophysics carries out leading research across the electromagnetic spectrum and using multi-messenger probes, including gravitational wave and neutrino astrophysics, and dark matter direct detection. Our research interests span a broad range of fields, from exploding stars in our own Galaxy, to accreting supermassive black holes and the shadows they cast, and the evolution of galaxies across cosmic time. 



We use observatories as varied as our research themes, from orbiting satellites like Fermi to undersea neutrino observatories like KM3NeT, to networks of antennas spread across the Earth like the Event Horizon Telescope. Closer to home, we are active users of South Africa's MeerKAT telescope, as well as the Southern African Large Telescope in Sutherland, and the High Energy Spectroscopic System in Namibia. We play leadership roles in next-generation telescopes, including the Square Kilometre Array and Cherenkov Telescope Array.  

 

The Wits CfA has a wide range of observational, theoretical, software, and instrumental experience, with an emphasis on the interconnect between. We actively pursue artificial intelligence enhancement of our research to stay at forefront of the data-intensive astronomy era.

Name: Helios
Description:
The global economy relies on critical materials:platinum, nickel, rare earths, lithium, cobalt.Without them, no batteries, no satellites, no defense, no digital systems.But these resources are becoming inaccessible:
– limited terrestrial reserves,
– unstable supply chains,
– massive pollution from each extraction.
Space, on the other hand, is full of these metals.And they are available there in astronomical quantities.It is the only long-term strategic reserve.

 Asteroid mining is not a distant ambition.It is a direct response to a geoeconomic bottleneck.

But here’s the problem: a mining mission takes 10 to 15 years.And in the meantime, most players burn through capitalhoping to survive until an uncertain “later.”

At Hélios, we did the opposite:We first launched a product that could be sold immediately.
Our turnkey satellites allow:
– profitability right away,
– funding our own R&D on mining,
– and most importantly, mastering orbital technologies starting today.
It’s the same platform.
The same technological building blocks.
But used for direct applications (observation, communication, space marketing, logistics) or strategic ones (military, orbital resilience).

So we asked a simple question:What if buying a satellite were as easy as configuring your car online?

At Hélios, we are rethinking space access with a simple promise:design, configure, launch, and control a satellite as easily as buying a car.

From our online configurator, each customer can create their custom satellite:module selection (observation, telecom, environment, logistics, private communication, etc.), design, colors, orbit, budget... everything is customizable.
A few clicks are enough to go from idea to liftoff.

Hélios takes care of everything, all the way to launch booking.

And once in orbit?Our interface allows simplified control from an app, with 3 modular communication modes:Direct ground, Constellation network and Global mesh network.

Name: New Technologies and Space Exploration in Commercial Law - Laboratory
Description:
By Order No. 5/2025 of 27 January 2025, the Dean of the Faculty of Law and Administration of Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń established a research team named: New Technologies and Space Exploration in Commercial Law – Laboratory (in Polish: Laboratorium Prawnohandlowych Aspektów Nowych Technologii i Eksploracji Kosmosu),  also known by the acronyms NTSECLL and LPANTEK.

The Laboratory is built on synergistic cooperation among its members and brings together: legal scholars, legal professionals, economics and management scholars, socioeconomic stakeholders associated with the Department of Commercial, Maritime and Civil Procedure Law (Faculty of Law and Administration, Nicolaus Copernicus University in Toruń) and student research societies (circles). 

The research areas of the Laboratory are: 1) new technologies, especially: artificial intelligence (AI), distributed ledger technologies (DLT), including blockchain, smart contracts and autonomous systems, including maritime, aerial, and space systems; 2) phenomena, in particular: space exploration and cybersecurity. 

The objectives of the Laboratory are: 1) conducting research and presenting its results, particularly through academic publications and during conferences and seminars; 2) supporting activities related to the evaluation of the quality of scientific activity in legal sciences by strengthening the impact of science on the socio-economic environment; 3) supporting the Polish administration in achieving its goals, including through expert participation in legislative processes.

Name: SEE Symposium and MAPLD Workshop is now SEE Symposium featuring SoCs for Space Workshop
Description:
Single Event Effects Symposium featuring the Systems-on-a-Chip for Space Workshop! (SEE Symposium featuring SOCs for Space Workshop).
While Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) have not gone away, we recognize that many FPGAs are SOCs and the trade space with other architecture SOCs is growing in the space regime.
The 2026 SEE Symposium featuring SOCs for Space Workshop will be held, May 11-15, 2026 at the Marriott Hotel in La Jolla, CA. The 2025 Symposia attracted nearly 200 professionals from Industry, Government and Academia. In addition to hosting 24 Exhibits, the event featured more than 30 technical talks, a poster session and a half day tutorial dedicated to SEE testing of FPGAs. Attendees represent fields of space radiation environment and radiation effects as well as those developing and using SOCs or SOC-based systems in space (e.g., FPGAs, GPU/TPUs, memories, power devices, communications, etc).
SEE/SoC will feature content including, but not limited to, the following areas:
SEE Areas:
-	Latest and greatest SEE test data
-	SEE Test Facilities (High Energy Heavy Ion, Heavy Ion, proton, neutron, …)
-	Pulsed Lasers
-	New models, tools, simulations and suites (design and analysis)
-	Test methods, best practices, guidelines, and standards
-	SEE Assurance and Mission Systems
-	Hardening / mitigation at system level
-	Fault propagation
-	Alternate SEE Test Platforms
-	In-orbit data
-	Training and workforce development
-	Data Sharing and Communication Platforms
-	SEE Space Environments
-	Special session on Wide Bandgap power devices and testing
-	Alternate SEE Assurance methods
SoC Areas:
-	SoCs, FPGAs, GPUs, TPUs, Neuromorphic processors
-	AI/ML, neural nets
-	SoC design, verification, and validation tools and methods
-	Standards, guidelines and best practices
-	Radiation performance
-	Security
-	Flight experience
-	2.5/3D packaging
-	Reliability, Assurance and test challenges
TBD Tutorial
For more details, visit our website: https://www.seemapld.org/.

Name: Associazione NEXABORN Group E.T.S
Description:
NEXABORN Group E.T.S. is an independent, non-profit hub of the Third Sector focused on innovation, scientific research, and technological development, with activities spanning the space sector and other strategically important fields.

The organization promotes advanced projects and sustainable solutions through interdisciplinary collaboration, scientific dissemination, and a responsible self-financing model. NEXABORN brings together professionals, researchers, and enthusiasts who voluntarily contribute to a shared vision aimed at generating concrete and positive impact in science and technology.

Established in accordance with the Italian Civil Code and Legislative Decree 117/2017 (Third Sector Code), NEXABORN operates on a voluntary and non-commercial basis. The organization does not engage in commercial activities or the sale of services; all resources are fully reinvested in research, education, and project development, in compliance with Italian law.

Participation in NEXABORN Group is free, transparent, and voluntary. All collaborations are regulated through formal volunteer agreements, reflecting the organization’s commitment to ethics, transparency, and long-term vision.

 Note: The official registration of NEXABORN Group as an E.T.S. is currently in progress and will be finalized once the required administrative and notary procedures are completed.

Name: Cosmos Ethiopia
Description:
Vision
To establish Ethiopia as a leading hub for Space Diplomacy and innovation in Africa, inspiring a new generation of leaders dedicated to the peaceful and sustainable use of the cosmos.
Mission
Cosmos Ethiopia is a non-profit organization committed to advancing Ethiopia’s emerging space industry and elevating its role in the global arena. We achieve this by fostering strategic Space Diplomacy, facilitating international Global Partnerships, and driving localized Youth Engagement to ensure that space technology is utilized for sustainable development and peaceful cooperation across the continent.
Our Mandate: Space Diplomacy for Sustainable Development
In an era where space activities are crucial for national progress and global security, Cosmos Ethiopia focuses on harnessing space as a tool for international cooperation rather than competition. Our core work emphasizes:
Peaceful Use of Space: We actively champion the ethical and peaceful application of outer space, contributing to multilateral dialogues that ensure a safe, sustainable, and accessible cosmic environment for all nations.
Global Partnership & Collaboration: We build bridges between Ethiopian institutions and prestigious international organizations, governments, and academic bodies. These partnerships are essential for knowledge transfer, capacity building, and collaborative research in space science and technology.
Advancing Ethiopia’s Space Industry: We work to integrate space-derived data (e.g., earth observation for climate, agriculture, and disaster management) into national sustainable development goals, ensuring that the space sector provides tangible benefits to the Ethiopian people.

	•	Collaboration: Partnering with global space agencies, private enterprises, and educational institutions to drive research and innovation.

--

Name: The New Zealand Space Health Research Network
Description:
By leveraging New Zealand’s research strengths in medicine, surgery, physiology, biotechnology and bioengineering, pioneering space companies, and advanced space infrastructure, this network aims to foster innovative research, unlock new opportunities, and elevate New Zealand’s position as a major contributor in this emerging field.

Focus and collaborative approach
The network will focus on addressing the unique health challenges of long-term space occupancy while also exploring the benefits these innovations can offer for terrestrial health. The same network aims to coordinate strategic research and foster deep-tech development in space health, seeding new companies that will contribute to the growth and impact of New Zealand’s space sector.

A key commitment of the SHRN is to engage with Māori partners, ensuring their voices help shape the network’s development. Collaboration and engagement are central to the network’s mission, positioning the SHRN to make meaningful contributions to space health and the broader NZ space sector.

Network activities
The Network aims to support the growth and impact of New Zealand’s contribution to the space health field by:
• Coordinating space health initiatives nationwide, to foster synergy and collaboration.
• Integrating Māori perspectives into space health research through a newly established framework.
• Creating pathways for New Zealand students to be involved in the global space industry.
• Engaging with both local and international researchers and commercial stakeholders.
• Facilitating coordinated interactions with major international space research entities (e.g. NASA and ESA).
• Fostering international collaborations and attracting funding for space health project.

Name: Legendary Rover Team
Description: LEGENDARY is a group of highly talented students of three different faculties of Rzeszow University of Technology: - The Faculty of Mechanical Engineering and Aeronautics, - The Faculty of Electrical and Computer Engineering - The Faculty of Chemistry. Together, we carry out a research project related to designing and building a Martian rover analogue, to take part in prestigious, international competition - University Rover Challenge. The event takes place at the research center, simulating the Mars environment (Mars Desert Research Station, Utah, USA). Apart from fulfilling the requirements of URC Organizers, we are guided by our own idea - in the future, we want our solutions to be applied in different areas of science and industry, eg. arms industry. New, ambitious students join our team every year in order to develop their interests and contribute to a cosmic project evolution. Our rover analogue is adapted to explore an unknown, difficult terrain (rocks, sand) and constitutes a basic research laboratory for soil analysis. Precise arm of the manipulator allows it to perform activities which an astronaut controls with only one hand.

Name: The SpaceSport
Description:
The SpaceSport is a blog combining two topics that seem to be worlds apart, but this page will show you otherwise!

Space technology is setting foot on all levels in sport, from amateur practitioners and grassroots clubs to the elite professional athletes. The SpaceSport aims to be on top of that game by gathering stories of space applications for sport in one place!

Did you know that astronauts on the ISS perform daily exercises with onboard fitness equipment such as a bicycle, a treadmill, and even a weightlifting machine? Ever heard of the space Olympics? What about out of this world records?

The aerospace sector is also known for its strong, lightweight materials and reliable technology. Such assets can offer added value to sports in a number of ways. From a cooling system developed for astronauts’ suits keeping Formula 1 mechanics cool, and enhancing prostheses in the Paralympics with materials originally used for rocket engines, and a spacecraft vibration control system stabilising skis. 

Not to mention the endless amount of satellite applications providing positioning services (e.g. elevation, routing, tracking) and environmental (e.g. weather, air quality, sea state) information relevant for the performance of practitioners on all levels, enabling also live transmission of sport events worldwide, and securing the safety of athletes at all times even in the most remote places.

Name: Space Policy Group
Description:
The Space Policy Group provides independent space policy analysis to help industry and government leaders navigate the increasingly connected global commercial and government space sectors. As the space market adjusts to the exponential growth in commercial space, we are seeing:

- The promise of new commercial capabilities capturing governments’ interest in new procurement strategies, expanded public private partnership models, and unique tax and subsidy arrangements.

- Global political and economic pressures reshaping government space budget priorities, driving workforce challenges, and realigning historic supply chains. 

- Orbital debris, in-space and lunar commercial activities, and human spaceflight safety concerns energizing regulatory activity in the U.S. and globally.

- Allied and partner nations seeing space, and the space industry, as strategic diplomatic and national security avenues.   

Whether you are a business or government leader, an experienced player, new entrant, or exploring the space market, the Space Policy Group will curate the right expertise for you to succeed in this dynamic landscape.

Name: SCARBOn -  Space CARBon Observatory next step
Description:
In response to the global goal to combat climate change and meet the objectives set in the 2015 Paris Agreement, accurate tracking of anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions and evaluation of their reduction efforts have become imperative.

Recognizing the need for precise data, the European Union has called for more detailed spatial and temporal GHG monitoring based on space observations.

Launching of the SCARBOn project in January 2024, a groundbreaking innovation action funded by the EU Horizon Europe programme, represents a clear answer to the challenges that lie ahead. The ambitious project is led by Airbus Defence and Space in Toulouse, France, and carried out by a diverse consortium of excellent innovation partners from six EU Member States. The project goal is to design a constellation of small satellites which will track carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4) emissions in the Earth athmosphere.

The SCARBOn satellite concept is built on the employment of the cutting-edge miniaturised static spectrometer sensor NanoCarb along with the aerosol sensor SPEXone.

Follow this page to find out more about the project!

Name: Duckweed Labs
Description:
Awarded by NASA | We are supported by a seed fund from NASA L’SPACE

Duckweed Labs is developing a compact Duckweed Protein Supplement Dispenser (DPSD) that leverages the plant’s rapid growth and high protein content to sustain astronauts on long-duration missions. This same innovation will soon support communities on Earth facing food scarcity.

===============================================================

Research & Technology 
We are creating a compact, low-maintenance solution for delivering tasty, fresh protein and nutrients in microgravity. This approach reduces reliance on costly supply missions, supports astronaut well-being, and aligns with NASA’s vision for sustainable exploration.

We are a dynamic, multi-institutional team with expertise in engineering, logistics, life sciences, and creative design. Led by Principal Investigator Thomas Liu, our group includes PhD candidates, military veterans, award-winning creatives, and medical students—all united by a passion for space exploration. Together, we are engineering an efficient, sustainable solution for astronaut nutrition, and beyond.

For more information, please visit the website!

Name: SpaceVeritas Lab
Description:
SpaceVeritas (or SpaceVeritas Lab) is a non-profit incubator focused on innovation and research in the space sector and other scientific fields. Our goal is to promote cutting-edge projects and technologies through collaboration, scientific outreach, and self-financing. We are an independent, non-commercial initiative that supports the growth of innovative ideas with a positive impact on science and technology. We bring together experts, researchers, and enthusiasts on a voluntary basis as collaborators to develop sustainable and advanced solutions, contributing to a better future for space research and beyond.

SpaceVeritas is established as a non-profit association under the Italian Civil Code (Law 383/2000), which regulates the management of associations and non-profit organizations. As required by Italian law, all activities of the association are voluntary and unpaid, with collaborators freely contributing to a shared and valuable project without expecting financial compensation.

The association is self-financed through funds from the founder's paycheck, in accordance with the principles set by the law, and not through commercial activities or sales. Any funds raised are used exclusively to support the activities and research projects, in compliance with Article 10 of Law 383/2000 and Legislative Decree 117/2017, which regulate volunteer organizations and non-profit associations in Italy.

Collaborators are aware that they are engaging in a free project driven by passion for science and technology, with the goal of contributing to research and innovation projects. No monetary compensation or reimbursement of expenses is provided, and the commitment will be governed by a volunteer agreement in accordance with the Italian Civil Code, Article 410.

Please note: the legal registration of the association is still in progress, awaiting sufficient financial resources from the founder to cover the related bureaucratic and notarial costs.

- Numerical advancements, and
- Material response,

Name: Lunar & Planetary Laboratory
Description:
Welcome to the University of Arizona’s Lunar & Planetary Laboratory (LPL).

LPL was founded in 1960 by planetary astronomer Gerard P. Kuiper, after whom the Kuiper Belt of icy objects in the outer Solar System and the Kuiper Prize (the top honor of the world’s largest organization of planetary scientists) are named. Most of the original work was based on observations using telescopes, but as the exploration of the planets expanded in scope, so did LPL. In 1973, the Department of Planetary Sciences was formed, to educate succeeding generations of planetary scientists.

Our dedicated faculty, research staff, and students come from a variety of backgrounds, including physics, astronomy, geosciences, chemistry, and engineering, but have a common interest in planets. In fact, the interdisciplinary nature of planetary science is one of its appeals for many of us, as we have to learn something of many of those other disciplines just to communicate, not to mention to succeed in understanding what is happening on, in, around, and between planets. Now, with the discovery of thousands of extrasolar planets, LPL is bringing that same interdisciplinary focus to understand whole new classes of worlds.

Regardless of discipline, students are empowered to contribute meaningfully to the design, development, testing, and delivery of extended reality (XR) products using virtual and augmented reality (VR/AR) technologies. Rooted in a commitment to interdisciplinary collaboration and Lean UX methodologies, the lab serves as a dynamic space where students and faculty converge to explore the endless applications of immersive technologies. 
 
The mission of OXRL is simple: empower student-driven product development through interdisciplinary collaboration while creating a safe and inclusive environment. Through a combination of hands-on experience and mentorship, students from diverse academic backgrounds are driven to explore innovative uses of XR applications. Students are equipped with the skills and confidence to succeed in the competitive landscape of tech innovation. It is an additional goal of OXRL to reduce or remove other complex barriers for students traditionally underrepresented in STEM fields, especially women. 
 
Hosted by the College of Innovation and Design (CID), the Open XR Lab is also home base to CosmoShox, our research and product development experience program focused on NASA-inspired design challenges. CosmoShox includes two main programs: NASA Spacesuit User Interface Technologies for Students (SUITS) and; NASA-funded research teams. Interdisciplinary student teams use a combination of Agile, design thinking, and Lean UX methodologies to build VR and AR products for specific NASA needs. Since 2020, CosmoShox has supported over 40 students from four academic colleges across campus.

Name: Critical Metals in Orogens (CMiO) 2024-2028 ANR Project
Description:
As the world shifts away from fossil fuels and enters an energy and ecological transition associated to the expansion of digital technologies, Europe needs to produce enormous numbers of wind turbines, solar panels, electric vehicles, and batteries and to regain sufficient autonomy for a pervasive digitalization.

Consequently, the European demand for critical raw materials (CRM) will skyrocket.

Europe is facing a major challenge: securing access to CRM by increasing the resilience of the supply chain and building a sustainable low-carbon economy. Indium (In), gallium (Ga) and germanium (Ge) are used in the production of solar panels and are essential to electronics and digital technologies (semiconductors, transistors, etc.).

We are on the cusp of a critical metal boom and therefore, we need to address urgently our approach to exploration and mining of CRM, otherwise the looming supply shortage in the coming decades will lead to a range of geopolitical, economic, and environmental issues.

The overarching aim of this project is to locate the new critical minerals resources vital for Europe’s future low carbon economy. As a successful and socially acceptable Energy Transition and Green Deal rely on responsible sourcing, we will focus on two objectives:

• O1: find value in new CRM resources in known base ore deposits & mines by unravelling the secondary metamorphic and tectonic processes that play a key role in concentrating CRM
• O2: unlock CRM resources trapped in mine wastes and engage in the CRM circular economy

Name: ParaLoon
Description:
ParaLoon develops inflatable / unfoldable "parabolic balloon" concentrator photovoltaic (CPV) systems to collect both photonic and thermal solar energy for applications in space and on earth. 

Applications in space would include satellites, space stations, habitats or mission vehicles. On earth, these structures could be used for remote areas, recreational / defense / refugee or other campsites, emergency situations or mobile applications.

The ParaLoon system combines lightweight, inflatable / unfoldable, parabolic reflector structures and different energy conversion technologies, e.g. concentrator photovoltaic (CPV) technologies, for the efficient conversion of solar energy to electric energy and optionally also heat. 

If wireless power beaming becomes a reality, the electric energy could also be transferred to users in space, on Earth or even on the Moon or Mars by satellites or a satellite network in the future.

Name: MUNStar-1 CubeSat
Description:
About Us:
MUNStar-1 is a pioneering initiative, marking its first year of operation as a student-led venture into the realm of CubeSat technology at Memorial. Our focus is on innovating and exploring the possibilities of small satellite technology across various sectors.

MUNStar-1 operates across the following critical subsystems:

Mechanical: This subsystem is dedicated to the structural integrity of MUNStar-1, focusing on the design, 3D modeling, and assembly of the satellite's frame to ensure proper fit and function of all components.

Mission Control System (MCS): Central to our operations, the MCS oversees all satellite subsystems. It handles the collection, storage, and management of data, ensuring the smooth operation of the satellite in Low-Earth Orbit.

Electrical Power Supply: This subsystem is responsible for powering the satellite. It includes solar panels and battery systems, designed to provide consistent and reliable power while adhering to safety and operational standards.

Thermal: Manages the thermal environment of the satellite, protecting it from the extreme temperature variations in space. This subsystem is crucial for maintaining the functionality and longevity of onboard components.

Communications: Ensures effective data transmission between the satellite and our ground station. It's key for receiving mission data, including scientific measurements and operational status updates.

Attitude Determination and Control System (ADCS): This system controls the orientation of the satellite, crucial for aligning its instruments and solar panels for optimal performance and data collection.

Join Our Team:
We welcome passionate individuals who are eager to contribute to this exciting field. Whether you're interested in engineering, space research, or project management, there's a place for you in MUNStar-1.

The future center will have a federated architecture, encouraging interoperability, resource sharing, reusability, collaboration, and knowledge management amongst groups interested in researching space applications, both within the department and beyond. The center will act as a central authority, focusing on the dynamics of economies of scale, standards, and wellbeing of the enterprise while providing the highest possible level of autonomy to individual member groups, providing them with access to centralized resources. Additionally, the SSC will also provide education in space systems both for residential learners and professionals and support the new MIT Aerospace Innovation Initiative with their focus on startups.

Name: Cal Poly Simulation Laboratory
Description:
Cal Poly Simulation Laboratory is a group of students working on projects involving modeling and simulation of aerospace systems.

Our activities encompass three main areas:

- Design, build and test systems to determine and control the attitude of 
   software and hardware simulations of spacecraft.

- Students develop skills in several modeling and simulation techniques, such 
  as numerical, Monte Carlo, biomimetic, and agent-based simulations, using 
  diverse coding tools and languages.

- Collaboration on position papers, blogs, and social media content to 
  promote peaceful, sustainable, and socially just applications of space and 
  space-based technologies.

By engaging in hands-on activities, students gain practical experience in modeling, simulation, design, build and test of spacecraft navigation systems. Students also participate in panel sessions, workshops, and forums centered on responsible space utilization and technology.

Name: Black Foundry
Description:
Black Foundry is redefining how spacecraft are designed in the era of abundant launch. While rockets have become fast and cheap, spacecraft design still takes 24–72 months—and that gap limits proposal responsiveness, slows innovation, and constrains national space power.

Black Foundry closes that gap.

Our platform is an AI-native, Python-first digital twin that turns natural-language mission needs into fully validated spacecraft and constellation designs. Requirements → components → simulations → SWaP → cost → CDR artifacts—all automated in a continuous design loop.

Before Black Foundry, I spent nearly two decades leading advanced mission design, digital engineering, and satellite development—including building the precursor toolset used for NNSA and DoD missions. Now we’re scaling that approach into a commercial system that accelerates proposals, increases pWin, and gives defense and commercial partners a strategic advantage.

From concept to CDR in hours, not years.
That’s the future we’re building.

Name: SpaceForTrades.org
Description:
Welcome to the new frontier for Skilled Trades!

SpaceForTrades is a volunteer-run initiative to forge pathways and connect tradespeople with opportunities for direct operational involvement in space, with key focus on space analog research, payload design/operations, spaceflight certification training, and eventual spaceflight.

Skilled trades are highly operational and training-centric in nature, emphasizing the practical application of science, technology, engineering, arts, and mathematics (STEAM) to solve problems, handle challenges, and produce competitive goods and services.

SpaceForTrades believes these characteristics make tradespeople well-suited for spaceflight and eventual off-world operations, and that including this diverse and powerful demographic will accelerate the endeavour of humans as a multi-planet and spacefaring species.

Name: Ethno-ISS
Description:
ETHNO-ISS is a pioneering research project in space anthropology — the first comprehensive ethnography of the International Space Station (ISS). It explores how humans live, work, and create meaning in the unique social and material environment of outer space.

By studying the ISS as a living, evolving society, ETHNO-ISS examines how astronauts, engineers, and mission planners build community, manage technology, and shape human culture in orbit. The project connects the fields of anthropology, space studies, design, and human factors, offering insights into what it means to be human beyond Earth.

Led by an international team of researchers, ETHNO-ISS contributes to our understanding of life in extreme environments, human–machine interaction, and the future of long-duration space habitation — from Earth orbit to the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

Name: ALFA Mars Terranauts
Description:
Welcome to the page of the ALFA Mars Terranauts, a team dedicated to exploring and studying the possibilities of a habitable future on Mars. Our team is composed of passionate scientists and explorers who are committed to uncovering the mysteries of the red planet and discovering new ways to advance our understanding of life in the cosmos.

At ALFA Mars Terranauts, we strive to showcase and engage in everything related to Martian life, including the latest scientific discoveries and cutting-edge research initiatives. As selected members of the team, we are thrilled to announce that we will be embarking on an expedition to Devon Island, Canada, a Martian analog, in the summer of 2023 to test out the possibilities of Martian life.

Our team is administered by the Foundation for Applied Molecular (ffame.org), a leading organization dedicated to advancing medicine using synthetic biology and exploring the mysteries of the cosmos. Founded in 2001 by a team of scientists at the University of Florida, Georgia Institute of Technology, and the University of Texas, ffame.org is committed to discovering new ways to understand life, what it is, and how to find it in the cosmos.

As a team, we are passionate about space exploration and the possibilities of a habitable future on Mars. If you share our passion and would like to learn more about our work, we invite you to explore our page and connect with us.

It operates with support from the Space Systems Laboratory  and the Department of Aerospace Engineering. SatDev’s goals are to document the CubeSat expertise collected by mission teams, advocate for expanded CubeSat development infrastructure, pursue new CubeSat projects, and organize opportunities for interested students to join CubeSat missions.

Name: Active Vigilance
Description:
Active Vigilance delivers innovative AI software solutions for aerospace and national security. Our edge applications  - optimized for low size, weight, and power requirements - are tailored to meet the demands of these highly specialized, demanding industries.

Our signature offering is a cutting edge AI fault detection and anomaly software tool, proven both in space and on the ground. It provides continuous monitoring, enhancing the resilience of autonomous systems and builds trust in the data they collect.  Our software enables operators to detect and address minor anomalies before they escalate into significant issues. In cases where spacecraft systems begin to degrade, the software informs operators in real-time, allowing them to determine the next course of action. Even in the event of a failure, Active Vigilance continues to provide valuable insights into the root cause of the issue. This proactive approach streamlines operations, optimizes human decision-making, and ensures precise outcomes, ultimately contributing to mission success.

Name: International Centre for Space & Cosmology (ICSC)
Description:
The International Centre for Space and Cosmology is a new initiative that will drive cutting edge research in the areas of Space, Astrophysics and Cosmology, train the next generation of researchers, and advance public understanding of this very exciting domain of Physics. and for the dissemination of important developments in these areas amongst the general public. It will also reach out to young students to encourage them to consider a research career in Space and Cosmology, and provide them with information regarding opportunities in these areas. While anchored at Ahmedabad University’s School of Arts and Sciences, it will work in collaboration with colleagues nationally and internationally with related interests.

The launch of the new Centre coincides with recent observations by the Event Horizon Telescope (May 2022) of the highly compact and dense object at the centre of the Milky Way galaxy. Using an array of networked radio telescopes around the globe, an image has been obtained showing the ultracompact object at the centre of our galaxy. The JMN (Joshi-Malafarina-Narayan) naked singularity model proposed and published in 2011 by Professor Pankaj Joshi, Director, International Centre for Space and Cosmology and colleagues contributed to the modelling that underlies this discovery.

Our mission is to strengthen Canada’s commercialization pathways by helping Canadian innovators move beyond lab validation through subsidized flight testing, data access, and domestic market readiness programs while also creating new opportunities for trusted collaboration with NATO and allied partners.

We have partnered with ConvergX and their Xpand Commercialization Centre to align with industry primes, ITB pathways, and global innovation networks that connect Canadian capability to international markets.

Chúng tôi luôn đặt chất lượng và sự hài lòng của khách hàng là tôn chỉ hàng đầu. Tại HPT Việt Nam, chúng tôi không chỉ cung cấp sản phẩm và dịch vụ tốt nhất mà còn cam kết hỗ trợ khách hàng trong suốt quá trình sử dụng và phát triển.

Description:
The astronautical Art Initiative is on a mission to build the most comprehensive archive of art in outer space, aiming to provide a historic overview of all artistic artefacts and interventions that operate beyond the boundary of space.


Inspiring Wonder, advancing knowledge:

Our mission is twofold: to inspire wonder by showcasing the creative spirit of humanity as it ventures beyond Earth's boundaries and to advance knowledge by conducting rigorous research into the impact and significance of space art. Through our work, we aim to bridge the realms of art and science, cultivating a deeper understanding of our place in the cosmos.

 
Why Art in Space matters:

Art in space transcends boundaries, encapsulating the essence of exploration, innovation, and the human spirit. It reflects our insatiable curiosity and our quest for beauty in the unknown. We recognise the profound value of art in outer space as both a cultural treasure and a source of inspiration for generations to come.


What we do:

Archiving & Documentation:
We curate a comprehensive archive of art sent into space, preserving these masterpieces for posterity and sharing their stories.

Research & Education:
We conduct research to unravel the artistic, scientific, and philosophical dimensions of space art, and we provide educational resources to inspire future generations.

Creation & Curation:
We create and curate group art projects in outer space, giving artists and the public direct access to space infrastructure as a source of inspiration and creation.

Name: LIDAL - Light Ion Detector for ALtea
Description:
LIDAL (Light Ion Detector for ALtea) is an Italian scientific experiment aboard the International Space Station, designed to study space radiation to enhance astronaut safety and support future human exploration. 

Developed as the successor of the ALTEA (Anomalous Long-Term Effects on Astronauts) project (2006-2012), LIDAL has been operating in ESA's Columbus module since 2020, within the framework of the Italian Space Agency (ASI) and in collaboration with University of Rome Tor Vergata and other partners. 

Since the end of 2024, an identical ground model of LIDAL has been installed at GSI Helmholtzzentrum für Schwerionenforschung (Darmstadt, DE) and is used as a reference detector for the Galactic Cosmic Ray Simulator, the first European facility capable of replicating space radiation conditions on Earth.

#LIDAL #ALTEA #SpaceRadiation #HumanSpaceFlight #ISSResearch

Name: SEMNAI Technologies, Inc.
Description:
SEMNAI takes its name from the Semnai Theai, ancient Athenian sister goddesses whose remit was to reconcile man's inhumanity toward his fellow man.  With an estimated 110,000,000 landmines in 64 countries and over 160,000 square kilometres contaminated in Ukraine alone, it would take more than 1100 years (assuming no further landmines were laid) to clear using existing demining methodologies, many of which date back to WWI. By bundling seemingly totally unrelated technologies, SEMNAI has calculated improved timeline clearance, remediation, and cost savings efficiencies approaching 80% with its disruptive platform. Breakeven on $7m comes with the successful pilot of a mere 3 square kms. Let's talk! 

SEMNAI is humanitarian technology designed to fundamentally change landmine and UXO clearance from the dangerous, 100-year-old manual, labour-intensive and cost-ineffectual methodology to a highly efficient, safe, remote multi-phase platform. Leveraging AI and Machine Learning to build a penultimate database of all known ordnance reaching back to WWII combined with GPR, GIS Mapping and cutting-edge SPEX technologies (a combination of remote sensing spectroscopy and polarimetry) SEMNAI will establish "profit-centre driven morality in aerospace".   

Recognising that our remediation efforts will invariably displace locally contracted demining personnel, our business model includes ESG and SDG models to reinvest 10% of contract values into microloans (inspired by the Yunus Foundation) for agriculture and hydrology redevelopment.

Name: Vulcan Propulsion
Description:
The Embry Riddle Aeronautical University (ERAU), Prescott, Liquid Rocket Program’s goal is to develop, launch to the Karman Line, and recover a reusable liquid propellant rocket. By means of various vehicle iterations such as Altair, Deneb, and Deneb 2, the program made advancements in propulsion testing by achieving a collegiate altitude record. However, ongoing technical problems restricted performance and reusability. Altair suffered an ignition caused explosion and valve/fitting failures. Deneb performed successful ignition and sustained flight but had shortcomings in engine efficiency, sensing of ignition, mass control, aerodynamics, and recovery. Deneb 2 was a repeat of subpar performance with the added feature of chamber ablation, and injector warping. These failures define three primary technology gaps: engine efficiency, robust ignition with abort/re start capability, and long-duration test chamber with thermal and mechanical degradation resistance.  
This capstone project will focus on the proposed engine which will be comprised of injector and chamber designs focused on increasing efficiency, a fluid-cooled chamber jacket permitting flight-like burn durations, and a torch ignitor for reliable and repeatable engine ignition. Achieving these objectives will improve program duration and provide a solid base for ERAU liquid propulsion teaching and research. Outcomes will inform future flight capable engines.

Name: octavoreto
Description:
Inspired by the EU Space Cybersecurity Hackathon held in Madrid in October 2025, the participants launched 8th Challenge (octavoreto) to stay ahead of future threats by developing quantum-resistant communication technologies that protect satellites, ground stations, and critical infrastructures.

octavoreto team is building a post-quantum cryptography (PQC) layer for the CCSDS communication stack, securing telemetry, telecommand, and payload data from ground to orbit. 

Designed for seamless integration into space-qualified onboard computers and ground stations, our modular, lightweight encryption provides end-to-end protection, authentication, and rollback security across S-band, X-band, and next-generation optical links.

Fully compliant with ESA and ECSS standards, our solution enhances resilience against both classical and quantum cyber threats. Built on open CCSDS standards and validated through ESA and ISISPACE missions, octavoreto transfers terrestrial cryptographic innovation into space — and beyond, securing mission-critical and terrestrial infrastructures with quantum-safe, space-proven security.

Name: UConn AIAA
Description:
Founded in 2015, UConn AIAA is a student-run organization split into two teams: Design, Build, Fly (DBF) and Propulsive Landing (PL). 

Design. Build, Fly specializes in designing a functioning model aircraft for AIAA's annual competition. Each year the parameters and special requirements for the project change. This year, DBF teams are required to fit in a two foot wide parking space and carry model people and medical equipment. DBF members utilize Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) modeling, XFLR5, and Ansys to model and simulate their designs. 

On the other hand, Propulsive Landing aims to successfully land a student-built and designed model rocket using solid rocket motors. The rocket launches from a pad on the ground, and once it reaches the peak of its flight a second engine kicks in. Code written in C++ and MATLAB helps to keep the rocket stable and oriented upwards in order to safely land. Simulink is used to simulate launches and perform virtual tests. The team is currently preparing for a test launch on December 2, 2023. 

As a whole, AIAA provides students with opportunities to hone their skills, learn new ones, and connect with other students and professionals within their desired fields.

Founded and led by Akshay Pandey, Founder and Managing Director, ARKANE is driven by a clear mission: to create an ecosystem where young innovators are not limited by age, geography, or traditional systems, but are empowered to pursue bold ideas with discipline and purpose.

At its core, ARKANE is not just an organization—it is a commitment to pushing the boundaries of what is considered possible, and proving that with the right mindset and execution, today’s students can build tomorrow’s breakthroughs.

Name: Tanzania Space Agency
Description:
Tanzania Space Agency (TSA) is a pioneering organization dedicated to propelling Tanzania's presence in the global space industry. As the nation's premier public space agency, TSA spearheads initiatives in space exploration, satellite development, and scientific research to unlock Tanzania's potential in the cosmos.

Driven by a vision of scientific advancement and technological innovation, TSA works tirelessly to bridge the gap in space capabilities, fostering international collaborations and driving domestic expertise. With a multidisciplinary approach, TSA focuses on cutting-edge research, space missions, and the development of indigenous space technologies.

TSA's mandate encompasses diverse areas, including satellite communications, Earth observation, deep space exploration, and satellite navigation systems. By harnessing space-based data and applications, TSA aims to address socio-economic challenges, promote sustainable development, and enhance national security.

At the core of TSA's mission is the nurturing of local talent and inspiring the next generation of scientists, engineers, and space enthusiasts. Through educational programs, internships, and partnerships, TSA aims to cultivate a skilled workforce and cultivate a passion for space science and technology across Tanzania.

As Tanzania's space agency, TSA collaborates with international space organizations, research institutions, and industry partners to leverage resources, expertise, and funding opportunities. By fostering collaboration and knowledge exchange, TSA strives to position Tanzania as a respected player in the global space arena.

With a commitment to innovation, exploration, and national progress, Tanzania Space Agency is propelling Tanzania towards new frontiers in space, inspiring generations, and transforming the nation's future through the limitless possibilities of the cosmos.

Through a blend of hands-on workshops, technical projects, and collaborative challenges, ANTRiX aims to cultivate a deep understanding of the space domain, while also promoting teamwork, problem-solving, and leadership skills.

The club is actively involved in organizing educational events like quizzes, hackathons, and competitions, along with sessions that dive into the intricacies of space exploration and celestial phenomena. Members of ANTRiX often engage in interdisciplinary projects that bridge theoretical concepts with practical applications, helping them gain valuable experience in areas such as simulation, coding, and system design.

By creating an environment that encourages creativity and curiosity, ANTRiX empowers students to pursue their passions in space and become the next generation of pioneers in the aerospace and astrophysics sectors.

Exploon

Name: SWIM - Surfing Radio Waves to Detect Liquid Water in the Solar System
Description:
Il progetto SWIM è all’avanguardia nell’esplorazione spaziale, focalizzandosi sulla ricerca di acqua liquida e sulle condizioni di abitabilità nelle croste ghiacciate dei satelliti galileiani di Giove: Europa, Ganimede e Callisto. Questo ambizioso progetto si inserisce nel contesto delle future esplorazioni spaziali, tra cui la missione ESA JUICE (JUpiter ICy moons Explorer), lanciata nel 2023, e la missione NASA Europa Clipper, lanciata a ottobre 2024.
SWIM si propone di sviluppare un laboratorio unico al mondo presso l’Università Roma Tre.
Questo centro di ricerca avanzato supporterà le osservazioni radar e le misurazioni che saranno acquisite dalle due missioni spaziali, analizzando in dettaglio i dati relativi alle profondità, all'estensione e alle caratteristiche chimico-fisiche dell’acqua liquida presente sotto le croste ghiacciate dei satelliti gioviani.

Name: Space Women
Description: Empoderar a mujeres y jóvenes a través de experiencias inmersivas en astroturismo y turismo espacial. Lideradas por mujeres STEM, queremos inspirar a la próxima generación de exploradoras espaciales mediante actividades innovadoras que simulan misiones, entrenamientos y la vida en el espacio, utilizando tecnologías como realidad aumentada, virtual y mixta, en entornos educativos y recreativos.

Name: Insulin River
Description:
We will pioneer the Alcubierre Turing warp drive and artificial general intelligence.

We will found a new field of science to accomplish these goals.

Holistic Consciousness Science" (HCS). This initiative aims to study consciousness through a collaborative effort across various scientific disciplines. Here's a summary of the proposed team composition and their roles:

Neuroscientists: To investigate the biological basis of consciousness, understanding its roots in brain functions.
Psychologists: To explore the nature of consciousness from a behavioral perspective, questioning why we perceive ourselves as conscious.
Philosophers: To address fundamental questions about consciousness, such as its nature and existence, possibly delving into thought experiments like whether a falling tree makes a sound if no one is around.
Physicists: To examine if consciousness could be explained by quantum mechanics or other physical theories.
Computer Scientists/AI Researchers: To explore whether artificial intelligence can simulate consciousness or if AI simply mimics consciousness.
Linguists: To investigate how language might influence or shape consciousness.
Biologists: To understand the evolutionary aspects of consciousness, potentially tracing its origins to simple life forms.

The initiative seems to emphasize the importance of communication across these fields to tackle the complex issue of consciousness, suggesting that the problem isn't just in understanding consciousness itself but in how different experts communicate about it. The playful tone (e.g., referring to consciousness as a "good party trick") indicates an attempt to engage a broader audience in this scientific endeavor, making the concept of studying consciousness more approachable and perhaps more relatable. 

This approach reflects a holistic view, acknowledging that consciousness likely cannot be fully understood from a single disciplinary perspective but requires a synthesis of insights from multiple fields.

Name: Bright Forge

Name: NGHIÊN CỨU ĐÔNG TRƯỞNG
Description:
Dong Truong Research: Transforming Data into Breakthrough Decisions, Driving Sustainable Growth

Name: Aerospace ADVERSARY Laboratory
Description: Cornell University's Aerospace ADVERSARY Laboratory (AAL) is pioneering new space architectures that will guide the aerospace sector towards fundamentally new space technology. We break things in order to redefine how they work, take big risks, and fail fast. AAL is focused on developing robust national security offensive and defensive technical capabilities for space systems while understanding their ethical and policy implications.

Description: At VisualAtles, we specialize in providing high-precision topography and aerial imagery at an affordable price. Our comprehensive range of services includes  photogrammetry, volume calculations, aerial photography, drone inspections, and much more.

Description: Nitre Space is a deep tech multi-industry consultancy at the forefront of offering solutions for space, quantum, and climate industries. Name: Global Space Services (GSS)
Description:
Global Space Services AB (GSS) is a Swedish company providing secure and high-quality ground station services for satellite operators worldwide. Through Hosting and Ground Station as a Service (GSaaS), we offer scalable and reliable solutions to support seamless satellite communications from prime polar locations.

We are committed to long-term satellite and spacecraft support, leveraging both our own facilities and strategic partnerships. Our advanced ground station infrastructure at the Northern Polar Satellite Center (NPSC) in northern Sweden is designed to serve polar-orbiting missions, reinforcing our position as a trusted choice for satellite communications.

Let’s connect to explore how GSS can support your mission.

Description: We perform research to enable sustainable innovative VLEO mission and application.

Name: AURORA

Name: EYE IN THE SKY TEAM
Description: We’re the EYE IN THE SKY team: Franciszek Badziak, Wiktoria Bortlisz, Jan Czałbowski, Klara Powierża, Franciszek Rzońca, Wojciech Szymczyk, and our tutor Piotr Morawiecki. Together, we designed and built a soda-can-sized satellite to locate people in remote areas like mountains and deserts. Our hard work paid off with 3rd place in the European Space Agency Finals. We’re proud to innovate for seach-and-rescue missions and make a difference!

Name: ENSMA Space Project
Description: L'ENSMA Space Project est un club relié à l'Ecole National Supérieure de Mécanique et d'Aérotechnique. Ces activités sont diverses et concernent les concours étudiants auxquelles peuvent participer les étudiants de l'ENSMA : cela va de la conception de fusée jusqu'à la participation au défis aérospatial étudiant organisé par le CNES.

Name: All-Star Agency
Description:
All-Star creates out-of-this-world opportunities for the technical leaders, deal makers, and thought leaders who shape the space industry.

Across all mission, orbit, and customer types – from telecommunications, to  remote sensing, to exploration – from Low-Earth Orbit to Geostationary Orbit, and beyond – from commercial to United States Government, and international customers – we represent the all-star engineers, software developers, technical writers, business developers, and more.

In service of them, we are committed to becoming the strongest, most vibrant, and most innovative service company in the industry.

At All-Star, every client is represented by the entire agency. This collaborative approach benefits our clients as they plan and build their impact across multiple facets of the industry.

With a global network and strategic partners in the United States, the Americas, Europe, Asia, and beyond, All-Star taps its unequaled ecosystem of experts, relationships, access, and industry intelligence to help ensure that our clients achieve their goals.

With a passion matched only by that of our clients, All-Star is committed to crafting precedent-setting deals and building new businesses driven by our clients’ ideas, interests, and opportunities.

Name: ema-enac
Description: The Electromagnetics and Antennas (EMA) research group is part of the Telecommunication team of the ENAC laboratory. This group carries out research and studies on electromagnetics and antennas. Its activity is mainly focused on the Communication, Navigation, and Surveillance (CNS) systems for civil aviation.

Name: Live Earth Imaging
Description: Capitalizing on the benefits of geostationary orbit, the Live Earth Imaging satellite constellation will be the first commercial Earth observation company to offer immediate image capture and continuous second-by-second imagery.

Name: AstroLots
Description:
AstroLots is committed to enabling the growth of the space economy by providing reliable on-orbit refueling services and facilitating the transportation of helium-3 from the Moon to Earth orbit. Our vision is to become the first and primary provider of bipropellant refueling capabilities while pioneering the delivery of helium-3 as a clean and sustainable energy source.

Through the design, construction, and operation of two key spacecraft systems—the Lunar Transporter (L) and the Orbital Transfer Station (OTS)—AstroLots will establish a robust infrastructure to support purely spacefaring vehicles. The L spacecraft will shuttle helium-3 between the Moon and low Earth orbit, while the OTS will serve as a refueling and transfer hub in Earth orbit. This integrated system will foster the development of a sustainable and efficient space transportation ecosystem, advancing humanity's reach into space and unlocking the full potential of the lunar economy.

Name: HowMuchSun
Description:
HowMuchSun aims to speed up the renewable energy transition in the Mediterranean, focusing on solar energy in Egypt. 
By using Copernicus data (CAMS and C3S), advanced modelling, and decision-support tools, it will help stakeholders optimise energy production, minimise losses from environmental factors like dust, and improve renewable energy planning. 

Supported by ECWMF, the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts, HowMuchSun integrates cutting edge Copernicus climate and atmosphere data in a now- and short-term forecasting service for the solar industry. 
HowMuchSun is a product developed under the ECMWF/CJS2_155b contract, 'Demonstrator Cases to Supporting the Renewable Energy Transition Across the Mediterranean'. The project is led by Evenflow (EVF), in partnership with the National Observatory of Athens (NOA) and CEDARE.

Name: VikramSat Student Satellite Project
Description: We are a group of students designing the next-gen satellite with advanced remote sensing capabilities. VikramSat will also demonstrate high data-rate laser communication link. Under ISRO'S Student Satellite Initiative.

Name: Cube3
Description:
Cube³ is a small satellite and spacecraft development club! We provide an opportunity for students interested in space to get hands-on experience working on various small satellites and nodes. We connect with other students interested in space, and professionals in the industry including speakers and alumni associated with NASA, and more planned.

Our club is developing Project JAVELIN, recently featured in the SJSU COE Alumni Magazine! It is a can-satellite, entirely produced by Cube³ and its mechanical, electrical, software, ground control, and rocketry teams! The satellite will be self-deployed and will collect flight data including temperature, pressure, orientation, acceleration, velocity, camera footage, and GPS, all while using radios to send this info down to us in real-time!

The project has seen 18 members throughout its teams, who have worked hard to create a can satellite that is ready for its first launch in April, only 6 months after its creation!

Name: iSenses Inc.
Description:
iSenses is a machine intelligence company providing products and solutions for autonomy, imaging, and digitization of assets, processes, and services. 

Our patented neocortex-based algorithm in near-real-time (≤ 10 ms) detects, tracks, learns, classifies, identifies, and ranges multiple objects, persons, events of interest (EOI) or anomalies. 

Applications are in the domain of automotive, aerospace, satellite downstream, predictive analytics,  and managed services. 

In the area of automotive, the noteworthy applications are advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), assisted driving (AD), simultaneous localization, mapping and path planning, detection of lanes and traffic signs in normal and adverse driving conditions of rainfall, snowfall, fog, haze, sandstorm, and occlusions, multiple vehicles, pedestrians, bicyclists, animals detection, driver behavior analytics.

Name: TECH TURF OFFICIAL
Description:
Tech Turf (TT) is a student-founded technology venture focused on aerospace, IoT, and smart innovations. Based in Coimbatore, India, we are passionate about building rockets, embedded systems, and life-saving consumer devices.

Our flagship projects include the Mark-series rockets, Rockbreaker V0.1, the Smart Hub sustainable energy system, and the Life-Saving Watch. Every prototype is designed with the mission to Innovate, Inspire, and Ignite.

Tech Turf blends aerospace research, electronics, and software into practical, deployable solutions. We aim to collaborate with students, researchers, and industries to scale bold ideas into global technologies.

 Headquarters: Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India

Name: SPACE UFABC
Description: A SPACE - Sala de Práticas Aeroespaciais: Culturais e Extensionistas é um projeto de extensão universitária da Universidade Federal do ABC (UFABC) que tem como objetivo integrar práticas acadêmicas e culturais no campo aeroespacial, promovendo a disseminação do conhecimento e a realização de atividades práticas para estudantes e a comunidade. O projeto visa aproximar os estudantes da realidade do setor aeroespacial, oferecendo uma plataforma para a troca de conhecimentos, experiências e desenvolvimento de habilidades técnicas e culturais. Por meio de workshops, palestras, eventos e projetos práticos, a SPACE busca fomentar a formação de profissionais preparados para contribuir com a inovação e o avanço das tecnologias aeroespaciais, além de proporcionar um ambiente de aprendizado interdisciplinar e colaborativo.

Name: Kritova SpaceTech
Description:
At Kritova SpaceTech, we are shaping the future of space technology by developing cutting-edge solutions in the satellite and CubeSat industry. Inspired by the Sanskrit word "Kritova", meaning "one who creates and innovates", we are on a mission to revolutionize space education, satellite consultancy, and advanced aerospace solutions.

We aim to:
 Provide expert consultancy for startups, universities, and organizations tackling satellite design, launch strategies, and mission planning.
 Deliver high-quality satellite components to empower the next generation of CubeSats and small satellite missions.
 Educate and inspire through courses, webinars, and hands-on training in space technology.

From an idea on paper to a real-life satellite orbiting Earth, we strive to bridge the gap between imagination and innovation, making space technology more accessible and achievable.

Name: Mearth Space Industries Corporation
Description:
Mearth Space Industries Corporation, short MSIC, is a subsidiary of the Project Moon Hut Foundation (A US 501(c)(3) Non-Profit Public Charity).

It only serves one goal: to plan, coordinate, build, and operate the 4 Phases of the Moon Hut Project to establish a box with a roof and a door on the moon, to benefit all species on Earth.

Name: Terralis Space Systems
Description:
Terralis Space Systems is an emerging aerospace organization focused on developing technologies that make space safer, cleaner, and more accessible. Our work begins with addressing one of the most critical challenges in orbit today : space debris, through the development of advanced, scalable removal systems designed to support long-term orbital sustainability.

Beyond debris mitigation, Terralis is also laying the groundwork for future in-space mobility. We are exploring the next generation of orbital transfer vehicles that will enable efficient movement between orbits and support a wide range of commercial and scientific missions.

Our goal is simple: to contribute to the infrastructure that will support a sustainable and thriving space economy for decades to come.
